coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic activity of ZnO sensitized by carbon quantum dots and application in phenol wastewater
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Huiqin
Tai, Xiumei
Du, Zhiping
Yin, Yanji
coredata.dc:description: This work reports on preparation of ZnO composites sensitized by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and its photocatalytic degradation on phenol under visible-light illumination. With the help of tools such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis diffusive reflectance spectra (UV–Vis DRS) and gas chromatograph-mass spectrometer (GC-MS), successful synthesis of ZnO composites sensitized by CQDs has been confirmed, which extend the spectral response range of the procatalyst to the visible region enhancing the utilization of sunlight. Meanwhile, transient photocurrent response and Nyquist plot analysis were further proved the increase in carrier separation efficiency of the procatalyst. The photocatalytic ability of the composites was measured by XPA-7 photocatalytic instrument with using phenol as simulate pollutants. And the photocatalytic reaction mechanism was also revealed by the Mott-Schottky plot, band structure and trapping experiments of active species. The results exhibit that the photocatalytic performance of ZnO composites sensitized by CQDs is 60% higher than that of pure ZnO with good stability and reusability, whose utilization has almost no significant change after 10 cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO
L-CQDs
Composites
Photocatalysis
Phenol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nanometer ZnO and cationic L-CQDs
UV–vis DRS studies and band gap calculation, transient photocurrent response and Nyquist plot analyses

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) (c) (d) TEM images of L-CQDs, ZnO and L-CQDs/ZnO composites; (b) particle size distribution of L-CQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of pure ZnO, L-CQDs and 3% L-CQDs/ZnO; (b) XRD pattern of ZnO and x%-L-CQDs/ZnO composites.
(a, b, d) Zn2p, C1s, O1s spectra for L-CQDs/ZnO, respectively; (c) O1s spectra for L-CQDs.
(a) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of ZnO and x%-L-CQDs/ZnO; (b)Tauc plot images derived from UV-DRS; (c)and transient photocurrent responses; (d)and Nyquist plots of ZnO and 3% L-CQDs/ZnO.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra at varying excitation wavelengths and UV–Vis Spectroscopy of L-CQDs. Insets are photos of L-CQDs aqueous solution under 365 nm UV lamp (left) and room light (right); (b) Fluorescence spectra excited with 400 nm laser for ZnO and x%-L-CQDs/ZnO composites.

(a) Effect of different L-CQDs loading on photocatalytic degradation rate of phenol; (b) Effect of 2% L-CQDs/ZnO concentration on photocatalytic degradation rate of phenol; (c) GC-MS determination results of phenol water samples under different illumination durations; (d) The repeated photocatalytic decomposition of phenol solution for pure ZnO and 3%L-CQDs/ZnO.
(a) The Mott-Schottky plots; (b) band structure diagram of ZnO and 3%L-CQDs/ZnO.
(a) Trapping experiments of active species; (b)mechanism diagram of photocatalytic degradation on phenol by L-CQDs/ZnO under visible light irradiation.
Fluorescence lifetimes (τ) and absolute fluorescence quantum yields (ϕ) of L-CQDs aqueous solution.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic activity of ZnO sensitized by carbon quantum dots and application in phenol wastewater

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Key Laboratory of Surfactants, China Research Institute of Daily Chemistry Co., Ltd, Taiyuan, Shanxi, 030001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China Research Institute of Daily Chemistry Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Key Laboratory of Surfactants, China Research Institute of Daily Chemistry Co., Ltd, Taiyuan, Shanxi, 030001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, Shanxi, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Resources and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, Shanxi, 030006, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Shanxi Key Laboratory of Surfactants, China Research Institute of Daily Chemistry Co., Ltd, Taiyuan, Shanxi, 030001, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China Research Institute of Daily Chemistry Co., Ltd

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports on preparation of ZnO composites sensitized by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and its photocatalytic degradation on phenol under visible-light illumination. With the help of tools such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis diffusive reflectance spectra (UV–Vis DRS) and gas chromatograph-mass spectrometer (GC-MS), successful synthesis of ZnO composites sensitized by CQDs has been confirmed, which extend the spectral response range of the procatalyst to the visible region enhancing the utilization of sunlight. Meanwhile, transient photocurrent response and Nyquist plot analysis were further proved the increase in carrier separation efficiency of the procatalyst. The photocatalytic ability of the composites was measured by XPA-7 photocatalytic instrument with using phenol as simulate pollutants. And the photocatalytic reaction mechanism was also revealed by the Mott-Schottky plot, band structure and trapping experiments of active species. The results exhibit that the photocatalytic performance of ZnO composites sensitized by CQDs is 60% higher than that of pure ZnO with good stability and reusability, whose utilization has almost no significant change after 10 cycles.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, industrial wastewater continues to be the largest source of pollution to the environment. Due to its complex composition and various types of pollutants, how to efficiently and quickly treat wastewater from different industries to meet emission standards is an urgent problem. Among of these pollutants, phenolic organics is the thorn in industrial wastewater [1]. Photocatalytic technology is a new energy-efficient modern wastewater treatment technology based on the photochemical reaction and catalytic reaction of the catalyst under light conditions. The core of photocatalytic technology is photocatalyst. Many materials have been used as photocatalysts, such as titanium dioxide (TiO2), zinc oxide (ZnO), tin oxide (SnO2), zirconium dioxide (ZrO2), cadmium sulfide (CdS), etc. and a variety of oxides, sulfide semiconductors [2–7]. ZnO is widely used in the field of photocatalysis by researchers due to its stable physical and chemical properties, non-toxic and harmless, low price, and good biocompatibility [8–10], which is considered to be a substitutable substance after TiO2 in the field of photocatalysis [11,12].

However, commercial ZnO as a photocatalyst still exists some shortcomings: on the one hand, the band gap is up to 3.37 eV [13], and only can be excited by light of less than 400 nm. This part of the light merely accounts for 5% of sunlight [14,15], which greatly limited the use scope of sunlight, and reduced the utilization of sunlight. On the other hand, commercial ZnO is so low quantum efficiency that the surface is easy to recombine photogenerated electrons-hole pairs. CQDs sensitization is an effective method to change the electronic density of bulk semiconductor materials and tune their optical and electrical properties [16]. As an excellent sensitized material, quantum dots can prevent the recombination of energy in addition to its sensitive visible-light response. Conventional semiconductor quantum dots mostly contain toxic heavy-metal ions, which have poor biocompatibility and seriously threaten the environment and human health [17]. Thus, many researchers attempt to find safer and non-toxic fluorescent nanomaterials. In recent years, CQDs, which consist of sp2/sp3 hybrid carbon atoms with a size of less than 10 nm and various functional groups on the surface, as a new type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, aroused spacious attention of researchers [8,16,18,19]. Compared with traditional quantum dots, CQDs have wide source of raw materials, low cost, stable physical and chemical properties, low-toxicity [20], good biocompatibility, easy functional modification, and anti-photobleaching [21,22]. In addition, CQDs have photo-induced electron transfer ability, photosensitivity and fluorescence up-conversion effects [23], which have potential applications in photo-electrochemistry and photocatalysis.

To date, various researches have reported ZnO/CQDs composite materials, and they were focused on the study of easily degradable organic dyes (such as Methylene blue (MB) [24,25], Rhodamine B (RhB) [26–28], Methyl orange (MO) [29,30], etc.). Ding et al. reported that ZnO foam/CQDs nanocomposites showed excellent photocatalytic activities on RhB, MO and MB under UV and visible light irradiation. The order of degradation efficiency is MB > RhB > MO [29]. Muthulingam et al. demonstrated a simple one-step method to prepare environmentally friendly (N–ZnO/CQD) composites, and three industrial dyes, malachite green, methylene blue and fluorescein were selected to investigate their photocatalytic performance. The results showed that the N–ZnO/CQD photocatalyst had good degradability to all three industrial dyes within 30–45 min [31]. These adequately proved that CQD doping modification can effectively adjust the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs of ZnO and improve its photocatalytic activity. However, a few investigations have been reported on ZnO/CQD as photocatalysis for degrading some small molecules and refractory organic substances (such as phenols and aldehydes). Herein, we prepared the ZnO nanoparticles with small particle size and photocatalytic activity using steaming ammonia method. L-CQDs were prepared by the pyrolysis of citric acid and ionic liquid. Then, L-CQDs/ZnO composites were successfully fabricated by the combination of L-CQDs and ZnO via hydrothermal method. The peculiarity and photocatalytic properties of L-CQDs/ZnO composites were systematically characterized by TEM, XRD, XPS, FT-IR, UV–vis DRS, GC-MS and transient photocurrent response and Nyquist plot analysis. And further investigated the photocatalytic degradation capacity of L-CQDs/ZnO by using phenol as a simulated contaminant. Thus, the work will contribute to ecological environmental protection, energy use and sustainable economic development.

Aminopropy-3-methy-imidazolium bromide, ([APMIm][Br], A.R. 98%) was purchased from Lanzhou Aolike chemical Co. Ltd. Ammonium bicarbonate, ammonia, phenol and citric acid were obtained from Tianjin Damao chemical reagent factory. Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), vitamin C (VC), and isopropanol (IPA) were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. The mentioned above reagents were of analytical grades and without further purification. ZnO was purchased from Liaoning Huludao Zinc Industry Co. Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nanometer ZnO and cationic L-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Nanometer ZnO was prepared through ammonia leaching process. Appropriate amount of ZnO was added to the 1000 mL beaker, followed by addition of ammonia water and ammonium bicarbonate in a certain proportion. The reaction system was filtered to obtain the supernatant after vigorous stirring for 4 h. The supernatant was steamed at 90 ± 2 °C until appeared a large number of white precipitate. The white precipitate was filtrated and washed with water in turns for three times, and dried in vacuum drying oven at 120 ± 2 °C, and then annealed at 500 ± 2 °C for 4 h with heating and cooling rates of 2 °C/min using a muffle stove in an air environment. Finally, the nanometer ZnO was obtained. Cationic CQDs (L-CQDs) was prepared following established procedures with modifications [32]. That is, the ratio of carbon dots in ionic liquids is optimized to 1:2.

L-CQDs/ZnO composites were achieved by mixing the nanometer ZnO and L-CQDs. Typically, 0.5 g ZnO was added in 200 mL beakers followed by an addition of 120 mL L-CQDs aqueous solutions with stirring for 2 h to form a homogeneous suspension. Then, the suspension was placed into a 200 mL stainless steel autoclave with a Teflon liner. The autoclave was heated preservation in air dry oven at 120 ± 2 °C for 2 h, and then cooled to ambient temperature. After the hydrothermal reaction, the white precipitate was filtrated and washed with water for three times. The obtained L-CQDs/ZnO composites were dried at 60 ± 2 °C for 12 h in a vacuum drying oven and then ground to fine powders with an agate mortar. The ratio of L-CQDs in composites is 1%, 2%, 3%, 4% and 5% respectively, with the composites marked as X%-L-CQDs/ZnO. The composites can be uniformly dispersed into water.

0.1 g photocatalyst of ZnO and X% - L-CQDs/ZnO (X = 1%,2%, 3%, 4% or 5%) was respectively suspended in 100 mL aqueous solution of 50 ppm phenol. The solution was stirred for 2 h without light to ensure the establishment of an adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Visible-light was simulated by using a 500 W Xe arc lamps. During the irradiation, 3 mL suspension, which was removed photocatalysts by centrifugation, was taken out at certain time intervals and analyzed on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 270 nm to record absorbance. Lambert - Beer law is followed by the concentration and absorbance relation of phenol solution throughout the experiment.

The obtained composites and L-CQDs were examined by HRTEM on a JEM-2010 microscope (JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV and electron gun of LaB6 crystal. The samples were made by dropping an aqueous solution onto a 300-mesh copper grid coated, which was dried under infrared light. XRD, which reflects crystal phase and composition of the sample, were recorded on a XRD-600 diffractometer (Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with 70 graphite monochromatized Cu-Kα (λ = 0.15405 nm) radiation in the range from 10° to 80° at a scanning speed of 8°·min-1. XPS data were obtained with a XSAM800 multifunctional surface analysis photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, British) using Al-Kα X-ray beam (1486.6 eV) with a beam current of 15 mA and operating voltage of 12 KV. The FT-IR were performed using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (VERTEX 70, 400-4000 cm-1) with the samples dispersed in KBr powder at 1:99 ratios and been tabulating. UV–Vis absorption spectra was conducted on a UV-1601 spectrophotometer, and the sample was prepared into a clear and transparent solution. UV–vis DRS (Cary300-800, Varian, American) was performed to test the light absorption capacity of the synthesized material by exposing a beam of light to a material table with BaSO4 as a reference, whose surface is only absorbed and limitedly reflected in all directions. Photoluminescence spectra (PL) of the samples was obtained using a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, F-4600) at 293 K. The absolute quantum yield of L-CQDs was determined using a steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (K. sol., C9920-02G) with a scanning wavelength of 350–1650 nm. The fluorescence lifetime of L-CQDs was measured using a transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, EdinburghFLS1000). Electrochemical analysis tests (Transient photocurrent response and Nyquist plot, Mott-Schottky plots) were performed on a CHI 660b workstation. The working environment includeed 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution and a typical three-electrode system with a working electrode (as-prepared sample was deposited on FTO as working electrode), a platinum wire counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode. Visible-light photocatalytic studies were performed on XPA-7 photocatalytic apparatuses with a Xe arc lamp.

TEM was used to characterize the microstructure and nanoparticles size of prepared sample. From Fig. 1 (a–b), we can clearly see that the obtained L-CQDs are irregular spherical structures with a particle size of 1–3 nm, and the average diameter is 2.1 ± 0.4 nm. The dispersion of L-CQDs is very uniform and no aggregation. The morphology of ZnO nanoparticles, which are prepared by steaming ammonia method, is the irregular granular shape with particle size distribution between 10 and 40 nm in Fig. 1c. And in Fig. 1d, L-CQDs can be seen from the surface of ZnO, which is demonstrated that L-CQDs successfully load onto ZnO surface by the hydrothermal method, achieving the fabrication of L-CQDs/ZnO composites.

FT-IR spectra further indicate that L-CQDs were loaded on ZnO. As shown in Fig. 2 a, an intense broad band at around 3446 cm−1 for all the samples can be assigned to O–H stretching vibration whereas a weak band at 1640 cm−1 corresponds to bending modes of O–H vibration [14,33]. And the characteristic absorption peak of ZnO at 430 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of Zn–O bond, and the 1384 cm−1 is associated with bending vibration absorption peaks of bridging hydroxyl on the ZnO surface. The FT-IR spectra of L-CQDs gives various peaks in 1770, 1384 and 1271 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of CO in the carboxyl group and the amido group, the bending vibration of C–N and the bending vibration of N–H, respectively. There are no fresh individual peaks in the spectrum of 3% L-CQDs/ZnO, indicating no new chemical bond generation. Moreover, the intensity of the Zn–O idiosyncratic absorption peak at 400-800 cm−1 of L-CQDs/ZnO composite was significantly enhanced, which demonstrated that there is a strong physical interaction between carbon and zinc atoms [34]. Thus, it was confirmed that L-CQDs are loaded on the surface of ZnO more than in the bulk phase.

The structure and crystal morphological characteristics of ZnO nanoparticles and L-CQDs/ZnO composites were investigated by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2b, it can be clearly seen that the seven main characteristic diffraction peaks of ZnO appear completely. These diffraction peaks correspond severally to crystal planes (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (112), and there are no other heterogeneous diffraction peaks. The diffraction data is in good agreement with that of the hexagonal ZnO (JCPDS No. 5–0664), which shows that the synthesized ZnO is of high purity and nearly no impurities. Compared to the ZnO, the diffraction peaks of the other five groups of different L-CQDs loading composites are closely identical with the ZnO, which indicates that sensitization with L-CQDs has a little effect on the crystal structure of ZnO.

XPS was used to analyze the surface chemical composition and bonding environment of L-CQDs and L-CQDs/ZnO composites. The core layer Ti2p, C1s, O1s spectra of L-CQDs/ZnO composites and O1s spectra of L-CQDs were represented in Fig. 3 (a–d), respectively. It is apparent that L-CQDs/ZnO composites contain C, O, and Zn elements. The distinctive peaks centered on 1021.1 and 1044.2 eV of L-CQDs/ZnO composites correspond to Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2 orbital splitting of Zn2p core level states, reflecting that Zn is completely present in Zn2+ form in the composite as showed in Fig. 3a [35,36]. While in Fig. 3b, there are two absorption peaks at 284.8 and 288.3 eV in the C1s spectrum of L-CQDs/ZnO composites, which should be attributed to sp2 hybridization of CC/C–C and COOR carbon atoms in L-CQDs feed stock, respectively. The O1s spectra of L-CQDs can be divided into two typical peaks of 531.6 and 532.6 eV, which demonstrated the presence of CO and C–O–H/C–O–C (Fig. 3c) [37,38]. Fig. 3d presents two fitting peaks of 530.25 and 532.1 eV in the O1s spectra of L-CQDs/ZnO composites, which are asymmetric and assigned to binding energy of O atom. Compared with the O1s spectra of L-CQDs, L-CQDs/ZnO composites are blue-shifted and the peak of O1s becomes higher, indicating that there is a certain interaction between L-CQDs and ZnO. And there is no obvious peak near 282 eV, which proved the absence of Zn–C bond. In our study, L-CQDs did not enter the ZnO lattice in a binding manner, but adsorbed on the surface of ZnO in a deposited manner.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis DRS studies and band gap calculation, transient photocurrent response and Nyquist plot analyses
ce:section.ce:para:
UV–vis DRS were employed to study the photo-responsive ability of the modified ZnO in the visible-light region. The UV–vis DRS of L-CQD/ZnO composites with L-CQDs loading at 1%, 2%, 3%, 4%, and 5% and pure ZnO were shown in Fig. 4 a. The pure ZnO nanoparticles have almost no light absorption with wavelengths greater than 500 nm. Different percentages of L-CQDs/ZnO have significant red shift compared to ZnO, and the absorption edge extends to the perceivable region. This is because not only L-CQDs itself can absorb light in the visible and near-infrared regions, but also there is a quantum confinement effect. When ZnO is loaded with L-CQDs, the small size of the L-CQDs allows the energy band to be broadened, and the position of the conduction band rises, which can make the energy bands be matched between ZnO and L-CQDs. The extended photoresponse range allowed photocatalysts to absorb more photons, promoting efficiency in harvesting visible-light [39]. However, the absorption intensity of the composites in the visible region is a peak distribution with the increase of the loading amount of L-CQDs, and 3% L-CQDs/ZnO composites have maximum absorption. With the corresponding, this also indicates that the photocatalytic activity of 3% L-CQDs/ZnO is best. The light absorption decreased with increasing of L-CQDs loading from 3% to 5%. This phenomenon is attributed to the fact that as the loading of L-CQDs increases, L-CQDs will agglomerate and lose the ability of the photoreaction of the catalyst, and the agglomerated L-CQDs loading on the surface of ZnO will affect the photocatalyst body to the illumination absorption. In addition, free g-CQDs can also act as the recombination center of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which will rapidly reduce its photocatalytic activity [40]. According to the UV–vis DRS data, the band gap of ZnO after L-CQDs sensitization was obtained by Tauc plot method [41–43], which were mainly based on the formula proposed by Tauc et al.:
(1) (αhν) 1/n = A(hν-Eg)
Where: α is the absorbance index; h is the planck constant, (6.626 × 10–34 J s = 4.136 × 10–15 eV s); ν is the frequency, (s); A is a constant; Eg is the semiconductor band gap, (eV); n is an exponent, directly related to the semiconductor type, n = 1/2 for a direct band gap semiconductor; n = 2 for an indirect band gap semiconductor, and ZnO is a direct band gap semiconductor. The calculated 3% L-CQDs/ZnO band gap energy is 2.96 eV (Fig. 4b), which is lower than that of ZnO. This also explains that L-CQDs can reduce the band gap of ZnO, broaden the light absorption range, and improve solar energy utilization.

The transient photocurrent response of ZnO and L-CQDs/ZnO further validates the increase in carrier separation efficiency. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 4c that the photocurrent response of L-CQDs/ZnO is significantly stronger than that of ZnO, demonstrating that the L-CQDs/ZnO has the admirable separation rate of photogenerated carriers under the visible light irradiation. Fig. 4d showed Nyquist plot of ZnO and L-CQDs/ZnO. The Nyquist plot radius of L-CQDs/ZnO is much smaller than that of ZnO, indicating that L-CQDs/ZnO has a faster electron migration efficiency [44,45], which responds to the UV–vis DRS and transient photocurrent response results.

In order to explore the fluorescence performance of L-CQDs, the fluorescence lifetime (τ) and absolute fluorescence quantum yield (ϕ) of L-CQDs were studied by fluorescence spectrometer. The obtained results were listed in Table 1 . Fluorescence lifetime is 7.37 ns and absolutely the fluorescence quantum yield is 9.05%, which is in a good position among the many carbon quantum dots studied [21,22,46].

Fig. 5 a showed the fluorescence emission spectra of 0.5 mg mL−1 L-CQDs aqueous solution at different excitation wavelengths and its UV–vis spectroscopy. The illustration is optical photos of L-CQDs aqueous solution under 365 nm UV light (left) and room light (right). It was observed that the maximum emission peak position shifts slightly to shorter wavelengths as the excitation wavelength increases (310–390 nm), and then to higher wavelength (390–450 nm), demonstrating that the photoluminescence properties of L-CQDs are excited wavelength dependent. The optimum excitation wavelength of L-CQDs aqueous solution is 390 nm, and the optimal emission peak position is 460 nm. The UV–visible spectrum (the red line in Fig. 5a) of L-CQDs aqueous solution have a distinct characteristic absorption peak at 354 nm, indicating the n-π* electronic transition absorption of the typical CO in L-CQDs, and there is a long tail in the visible region, which is the key to L-CQDs sensitization and improve the visible light absorption range of ZnO. As it can be seen from the illustration, the aqueous solution of L-CQDs emits bright blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp.

The optical properties of photocatalysts are closely related to fluorescence intensity. Photoluminescence spectra can be analyzed the separation and migration efficiency between sample carriers. The photoluminescence spectrum of L-CQDs/ZnO composite was shown in Fig. 5b. Pure ZnO produces two fluorescence at 451, 468 nm at 400 nm excitation, which is produced by free exciton recombination in pure ZnO. After modification with different percentages of L-CQDs, 3% L-CQDs/ZnO exhibits the weakest fluorescence, indicating a longer carrier lifetime and a lower photogenerated electron-hole pair recombination ratio. This result can be attributed to the efficient electron transport caused by the π bond conjugate system of L-CQDs.

Fig. 6 a plotted the phenol degradation rate versus reaction time under different L-CQDs loading. Degradation rate of phenol by L-CQDs/ZnO composites is significantly higher than that of pure ZnO nanoparticles, which improved nearly up to 60%. Interestingly, as the L-CQDs loading increased from 1% to 5%, the photocatalytic effect of the composites showed a phenomenon of first strengthening and then weakening. And the best photocatalytic degradation is 3% L-CQD/ZnO composites, which echoes its best visible light and weakest fluorescence absorption. The reason is probably that some of the L-CQDs are agglomerated, thereby losing its effect on the photocatalytic performance gain as the L-CQDs loading increases. What's more, the excessive loading of L-CQDs hinders the light capturing and charge transfer ability of the surface of pure ZnO nanoparticles [47], so that the photocatalytic performance is lowered.

The effect of concentration on phenol degradation rate was examined by selecting 3%- L-CQDs/ZnO as the optimal photocatalyst. In Fig. 6b, the relative concentration of phenol decreased from 53.4% to 0.3% as the concentration of 3%-L-CQDs/ZnO varied from 0.4 g L−1 to 1.0 g L−1. And then, when the content of 3%- L-CQDs/ZnO is further increased to 1.2 g L−1, the relative concentration of phenol is also increased to 47.6%, which indicates that 3%-L-CQDs/ZnO concentration affects the phenol degradation rate. The key to this phenomenon is the case that too much photocatalyst will hinder its binding channel to phenol. In order to accurately determine the phenol content in the photocatalytic process, the phenol water sample under different illumination duration was measured by GC-MS. The results are shown in Fig. 6c. The phenol peak intensity gradually diminishes as the illumination time increases, indicating that the phenol content gradually decreases. This result was also consistent with the UV–visible measurement.

In order to further studies the recyclability of the composites, the repeated experiments of photocatalytic degradation were performed by collecting 3% L-CQDs/ZnO composites with the best catalytic effect and pure ZnO nanoparticles. The results are shown in Fig. 6d. After 10 cycles, the photocatalytic performance of ZnO nanoparticles decreased significantly, while 3% L-CQDs/ZnO composites still had stable photocatalytic activity, and there was no significant decrease in the degradation rate of phenol aqueous solution. This is caused by the self-cleaning ability of L-CQDs [48,49] and be proved that 3%L-CQDs/ZnO composites had excellent photocatalytic stability, and L-CQDs sensitization is an effective method to improve the photocatalytic performance of ZnO nanoparticles.

As a usual metal oxide semiconductor photocatalyst, ZnO has a discontinuous energy band structure, which is composed of a valence band (VB) and a conduction band (CB). The energy gap between CB and VB is called forbidden band. The band gap of ZnO can be adjusted by loading L-CQDs with good electron transfer ability and extensive absorption of visible light to broaden the absorption range of sunlight. When a sufficiently towering light energy (greater than or equal to the energy gap between the CB and VB) of photocatalyst is imparted, the electrons in the VB are excited and enter the CB through the forbidden band, thereby producing photogenerated electrons in the CB, and forming photogenerated holes on the VB. Mott-Schottky plot was utilized to characterize the CB potentials of ZnO and L-CQDs/ZnO. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the positive slopes for the tested samples indicate that both are n-type semiconductors. And flat band potentials (EFB) of ZnO and L-CQDs/ZnO obtained from the tangent intercept of X-axis are −0.27 V and −0.52 V vs Ag/AgCl, respectively. Thus, the calculated ECB values are −0.47 and −0.72 V vs Ag/AgCl based on ECB = EFB - 0.2 [50,51], which are equal to −0.27 and −0.52 V vs NHE, respectively. Namely, EVB of ZnO and L-CQDs/ZnO are calculated to be 2.99 and 2.44 V from ECB + Eg. Based on the above results, the energy level diagrams of ZnO and L-CQDs/ZnO were obtained (Fig. 7b). Obviously, the ECB of ZnO did not reach the reduction level of O2/·O2 −, while the ECB positions of L-CQDs/ZnO stride over ·O2 − reduction level for phenol degradation. In addition, compared to the ·OH/H2O potential (2.27 V vs NHE at pH = 7) [44,52], the EVB of ZnO and L-CQDs/ZnO are both positive enough to drive the oxidation process of H2O to form ·OH due to their sufficient oxidation ability. Meanwhile, the EVB of L-CQDs/ZnO is more positive than those of ZnO, which is favorable for the photogenerated hole with a stronger oxidation potential, indicating that the L-CQDs/ZnO can exhibit more excellent photocatalytic degradation activity than ZnO. In order to clarify the mechanism, reactive oxygen species capture experiments were performed during the photocatalytic degradation process of phenol. Disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA, 0.01 M), vitamin C (VC, 0.01 M), and isopropanol (IPA, 0.01 M) as capture agents were added to the phenol aqueous solution to capture photogenerated holes (h+), ·O2 −, and ·OH, respectively. As can be seen from Fig. 8 a, the relative residual concentration of phenol can still reach 0.228, 0.283 with the addition of EDTA、VC, indicating that h+ and ·O2 − play a little role in the degradation process. However, the degradation rate of phenol is very small after IPA was introduced, and the relative residual concentration is as high as 0.883, which is caused by the ·OH captured by IPA. The results showed that ·OH is the key factor for organic degradation compared with h+ and ·O2 −.

Based on the above research results, a possible photocatalytic mechanism of phenol by L-CQDs/ZnO was proposed. During the photocatalytic reaction, the photogenerated electrons with very strong reducing ability, can convert O2 into ·O2 −. At the same time, the photogenerated hole can capture the electrons of the hydroxyl radical OH− or H2O on the surface of the material to form ·OH [53,54]. ·OH have elevated energy to degrade phenol into water, carbon dioxide and other compounds. For the organic substance containing the benzene ring, the photogenerated hole can also directly oxidize and open the benzene ring to achieve the purpose of degradation. The possible degradation pathways for phenol can be expressed by the following reactions and mechanism diagram (Fig. 8b).
(2) L-CQDs/ZnO + hv → L-CQDs/ZnO + h+ + e− (Eλ>Eg)
(3) e− + O2 + H+→.O2-
(4)
.O2 - + C6H6O→ CO2 + H2O
(5) h+ + H2O / OH- → ·OH + H+
(6) h+ + C6H6O→ CO2 + H2O
(8) ·OH + C6H6O→ CO2 + H2O

Given all this, ZnO nanoparticles and L-CQDs/ZnO composites with photocatalytic activity were successfully prepared by steaming ammonia method and hydrothermal method and the L-CQDs/ZnO composites over degradation of phenol was systematically investigated under photocatalytic conditions. The structure of the synthesized ZnO is hexagonal, and L-CQDs are successfully loaded onto the surface of ZnO via the strong physical interaction between L-CQDs and ZnO, which is physically adsorbed, not affecting the crystal structure of ZnO. The UV–vis diffusive reflectance spectra and fluorescence spectra show that the synthesized composites L-CQDs/ZnO also has significant absorption in the visible region. Transient photocurrent response measurement was revealed that the carrier separation efficiency in L-CQDs/ZnO is higher than that of pure ZnO. And Nyquist plot analysis showed that L-CQDs/ZnO has faster electron migration efficiency, which means the higher the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and the better the photocatalytic activity. Trapping experiments of active species showed that ·OH is the major active species throughout the reaction. Interestingly, composites L-CQDs/ZnO can completely degrade phenol at 5.5 h, and the phenol removal rate is improved by nearly 60% compared with the unsensitized ZnO. It is that the adsorption of L-CQDs on the surface of ZnO could transfer the charge from ZnO to L-CQDs, which enhances the absorption and utilization of sunlight by ZnO. Thus, L-CQDs/ZnO composites is also expected to be used to degrade the same type of phenolic organics, which provides theoretical direction and feasible measures for wastewater treatment and environmental protection.

Huiqin Liang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Xiumei Tai: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation. Zhiping Du: Resources, Supervision, Data curation. Yanji Yin: Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1610222) and the National Key Research & Development Plan (2017YFB0308704). We would like to thank the project of JALA Research Funds (JALA2017).
